skill.name: seo-handler
skill.description: Guide Next.js teams through SEO metadata, sitemap, robots.txt, JSON-LD, and ISR setup.
skill.summary: Apply practical SEO patterns for Next.js app routes and content pages.
skill.author: AayushBaniya2006

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many shell execution, network, environment, weak cryptography, and obfuscation patterns. Review found the shell and weak cryptography hits are false positives caused by markdown backticks and SEO terms, while the only confirmed concern is guidance that uses raw JSON-LD with dangerouslySetInnerHTML without warning about untrusted data.

medium_findings.title: Raw JSON-LD Injection Pattern Requires Trusted Data
medium_findings.description: The skill recommends rendering JSON-LD with dangerouslySetInnerHTML. This is a common SEO pattern, but it can create XSS risk if values are built from untrusted user content without escaping or validation.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact React dangerous HTML sink appears in both documentation files. The risk depends on whether consuming projects pass trusted or untrusted values into the JSON-LD object.

low_findings.title: Static Shell Execution Findings Are Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported Ruby or shell backtick findings are inline markdown code spans and fenced code examples. No evidence found of executable shell commands in the skill files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines contain documentation references such as file paths and code blocks, not runtime command execution. The skill declares only Read, Write, and Edit tools.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL Findings Are SEO Examples
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL findings are sample canonical URLs, schema.org context URLs, and example image URLs. No evidence found that the skill sends data to those URLs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs appear inside static SEO and structured data examples. They are not used in fetch calls, webhooks, or credential transfer logic.

low_findings.title: Environment Variable Reference Is Public Configuration
low_findings.description: The skill shows process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_APP_URL for a sitemap URL in robots.txt. This is a public Next.js environment value and is not evidence of secret access or environment file reading.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The variable name uses the NEXT_PUBLIC prefix and is used only to construct a public sitemap URL. No evidence found of reading .env files or exfiltrating secrets.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Findings Are Textual False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography findings align with SEO documentation text such as metadata and descriptions. No evidence found of MD5, SHA1, DES, or other cryptographic APIs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are metadata descriptions and OpenGraph fields, not cryptographic code. The files are markdown guidance, not executable libraries.

low_findings.title: Critical Dangerous Combination Heuristic Not Confirmed
low_findings.description: The static heuristic combined code execution, network, and credential access signals. Manual review found no executable code path, no network exfiltration, and no credential access in the skill content.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The underlying signals are explained by markdown formatting, example URLs, and a public environment variable example. No prompt injection text or malicious workflow was found.

dangerous_patterns.title: dangerouslySetInnerHTML Used For Structured Data
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes raw JSON-LD examples using dangerouslySetInnerHTML. Users should restrict this pattern to trusted serialized objects and avoid unsanitized user input.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The dangerous sink is directly present in the examples. The pattern is not malicious, but it needs clear data trust boundaries.

content.value_statement: Next.js apps often miss search visibility because metadata, sitemaps, robots rules, and structured data drift across routes. This skill gives focused guidance for consistent SEO implementation in Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
actual_capabilities: Explains where to define default and page-level Next.js metadata.
Shows how to add public routes to sitemap configuration.
Provides examples for split sitemaps on large sites.
Guides JSON-LD setup with next-seo components and raw script tags.
Recommends ISR settings for content-heavy pages.
Shows robots.txt rules for public and private route handling.
limitations: Focused on Next.js app-router SEO patterns, not every web framework.
Does not validate live search rankings or crawl results.
Raw JSON-LD examples require trusted or sanitized data.
Does not include automated tests or executable audit scripts.

use_cases.title: Launch a Marketing Page
use_cases.description: Add metadata, OpenGraph fields, sitemap entries, and crawl rules for a new public route.

use_cases.title: Improve Content Indexing
use_cases.description: Apply ISR, structured data, and route metadata for blogs, docs, and product pages.

use_cases.title: Review SEO Coverage
use_cases.description: Check a Next.js project for missing sitemap entries, robots gaps, and inconsistent metadata.

prompt_templates.title: Check a New Route
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my new Next.js route for SEO basics. Check metadata, sitemap inclusion, OpenGraph fields, and robots behavior.

prompt_templates.title: Add Structured Data
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me add JSON-LD structured data to this Next.js page. Prefer next-seo when possible and explain any trust boundary for raw JSON-LD.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Large Sitemap Support
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a sitemap strategy for a Next.js site with more than 50,000 product URLs. Include batching, lastModified values, and route placement.

prompt_templates.title: Audit SEO Architecture
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit this Next.js app for technical SEO architecture. Review metadata inheritance, ISR usage, sitemap coverage, structured data, and robots rules.

output_examples.input: Review a new blog page for SEO readiness.
output: Confirms page-level title, description, and OpenGraph image requirements.
Identifies the sitemap entry and expected lastModified source.
Recommends Article JSON-LD and ISR settings for crawlable content.

output_examples.input: Create robots.txt guidance for a SaaS app.
output: Allows public marketing pages for crawlers.
Disallows private app, admin, and API routes.
Links the sitemap using the public application URL.

output_examples.input: Plan SEO for a large product catalog.
output: Splits product URLs into sitemap batches below search engine limits.
Uses product update times for lastModified values.
Keeps canonical product URLs consistent with the base site URL.

best_practices: Keep default metadata in layouts and override route-specific values in pages.
Use structured data components when available before raw script injection.
Keep sitemap, robots rules, and public route changes in the same review.
anti_patterns: Adding a public route without a sitemap entry.
Using raw JSON-LD with untrusted user content and no escaping plan.
Blocking important marketing or content routes in robots.txt.

faq.question: Does this skill run an SEO crawler?
faq.answer: No. It provides implementation guidance for Next.js SEO files and page patterns.

faq.question: Can it edit my Next.js files?
faq.answer: Yes, the skill declares Read, Write, and Edit tools for project file updates.

faq.question: Does it support Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Is raw JSON-LD always safe?
faq.answer: No. Use trusted data or sanitize values before placing JSON-LD in a script tag.

faq.question: Is this only for Next.js?
faq.answer: The examples and file paths are written for Next.js app-router projects.

faq.question: Does it guarantee higher rankings?
faq.answer: No. It improves technical SEO setup but cannot guarantee search ranking changes.
